content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 14, 2012

Jim Thomas, Airports Manager
Tuolumne County Airports
10723 Airport Road
Columbia, CA 95310

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-12-149

Dear Mr. Thomas:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as Tuolumne County Airports Manager under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Moreover, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place.

Please note our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act (Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014). We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

Are you prohibited from working on the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) residential through-the-fence-required-access agreement (RTTF agreement) between Tuolumne County and residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport, when you are one of the residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport who will enter into the RTTF agreement.

CONCLUSION

Because you will be subject to the agreement in your private capacity you may not participate in your governmental capacity on the development of an RTTF agreement on behalf

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-12-149
Page No. 2

of Tuolumne County if the financial effect of the decision on your personal interests will be at
least $250.

FACTS

You are the Tuolumne County Airports Manager and in that capacity manage two
airports that are owned and operated by Tuolumne County. You are currently working on
understanding the recent changes to the Federal Aviation Administration's residential through-
the-fence policy.² Previously, the FAA had a policy against RTTF operations at airports that are
eligible for FAA Airport Improvement Program grant funding. This year Congress passed the
FAA Modernization and Reform Act that resulted in the FAA redrafting the RTTF policy to
allow RTTF airports to obtain FAA grant funding so long as they enter into an RTTF access
agreement with the residents who have such access.

One of the two airports you manage as Tuolumne County Airports Manager is the Pine
Mountain Lake Airport (PLMA) located in Groveland, California. The PLMA has a residential
airpark adjacent to it and is considered by the FAA to have RTTF operations. Tuolumne County
is interested in obtaining FAA grant funding for improving the PLMA under the new RTTF
policy. You have been asked to gather information about the recent policy change and develop
an RTTF agreement. The RTTF agreement will be submitted to the Airport Advisory
Committee, and if approved, will be considered by the Tuolumne County Board of Supervisors
for adoption.

You are also a property owner and resident of the airpark directly adjoining the PLMA.
You are concerned that you may have a conflict of interest in working on behalf of Tuolumne
County to develop the RTTF agreement that must be executed by residents in order for the
PLMA to become eligible for FAA grant funding. In essence, you will develop the required
RTTF access agreement for PLMA and also will be a resident who will agree to the terms of the
agreement. You note that one of the FAA's requirements is that each resident pay an access fee
for the privilege of accessing the airport and your responsibilities may require your input on fee
amount and type. You ask specifically about conflicts of interest at the following stages:

(1) Gathering publically accessible information pertinent to the project,
(2) Meeting with agency staff to develop policy options for a potential agreement,
(3) Drafting proposed agreements,
(4) Presenting the proposed agreements and policy to your Board of Supervisors as the
decision maker,
(5) Executing an agreement, as a resident, to maintain your private access to the airport.

² According to the FAA website, "Residential through-the-fence (RTTF) agreements allow people who
own residential property with aircraft storage facilities near an airport to access the airport from off-airport property.
These properties are sometimes known as 'hangar homes.' On February 14, 2012, the FAA Modernization and
Reform Act of 2012 was signed into law (P.L. 112-95). Section 136 of this law permits general aviation airports, as
defined by the statute, to enter into residential through-the-fence agreements with property owners or association
representing property owners."

File No. A-12-149
Page No. 3


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.

Steps 1: Are you a public official?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency. As the Tuolumne County Airports Manager,
you are a public official.

Steps 2: Will you be making, participating in making, or using your official position to
influence a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)

A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the
official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)

You stated that you would be: (1) gathering publically accessible information pertinent
to the project, (2) meeting with agency staff to develop policy options for a potential agreement,
(3) drafting the proposed agreements, and (4) ultimately presenting the proposed agreements and
policy to your Board of Supervisors as the decision maker. This conduct would be
"participation" in the governmental decision, absent significant substantive or intervening
review.³

³ Although you describe two layers of approval of the agreement (the advisory committee and board of
supervisors), your facts do not indicate that there will be "significant substantive review." The Commission has
historically interpreted this exception narrowly. In the Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-82-108 we stated: "It is our
interpretation that [an official] participates in a decision, even if it is 'reviewed' by superiors of his superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the [official] without checking it independently, if they rely on the
professional judgment of the [official], or if the [official] in some other way actually may influence the final
decision. Making recommendations to the final decision-makers, either directly or indirectly, is exactly what
participating in a governmental decision means."

File No. A-12-149
Page No. 4

You also ask about your personal decision to execute an RTTF agreement to maintain
your private access to the airport. This is not a "governmental decision" although you should
consult with your agency's counsel to determine if other laws such as Section 1090 might apply.

Step 3: What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
certain enumerated economic interests of a public official (See Section 87103.). In this case, part
of your duties will be to consider an appropriate fee to be imposed for airport access under the
RTTF agreement. An official is always considered to have an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the
"personal financial effects" rule." (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Steps 4, 5 and 6: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the RTTF
agreement and will the effect be material and material?

"A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her
personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18704.5.)

"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his or her
immediate family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-
month period." (Regulation 18705.5.)

You stated that you are a resident that will be required to sign the RTTF agreement and
pay the fee under the agreement to maintain airport access. At this point, we do not know what
the amount of the fee will be, but if it is more than $250 in a year, the effect of the decision will
be material.

Steps 7 and 8: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the conflict-
of-interest rules?

The Act provides two exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules:

• Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner
that is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public
generally (in your case Tuolumne County). (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

• Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying

⁴ We do not analyze the other economic interests under the Act since you did not provide facts suggesting
that your real property, business, or sources of income or gifts would be financially affected by the decision. If this
were the case, you should seek additional advice.

File No. A-12-149
Page No. 5

conflict of interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the "legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to your circumstances, so we will not address them further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.question:
Are you prohibited from working on the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) 
residential through-the-fence-required-access agreement (RTTF agreement) between Tuolumne 
County and residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport, when you are one of the 
residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport who will enter into the RTTF agreement.
sections.conclusion:
Because you will be subject to the agreement in your private capacity you may not 
participate in your governmental capacity on the development of an RTTF agreement on behalf  
of Tuolumne County if the financial effect of the decision on your personal interests will be at 
least $250.
sections.facts:
You are the Tuolumne County Airports Manager and in that capacity manage two 
airports that are owned and operated by Tuolumne County. You are currently working on 
understanding the recent changes to the Federal Aviation Administration's residential through­
the-fence policy.2 Previously, the FAA had a policy against RTTF operations at airports that are 
eligible for FAA Airport Improvement Program grant funding. This year Congress passed the 
FAA Modernization and Reform Act that resulted in the FAA redrafting the RTTF policy to 
allow RTTF airports to obtain FAA grant funding so long as they enter into an RTTF access 
agreement with the residents who have such access. 
One of the two airports you manage as Tuolumne County Airports Manager is the Pine 
Mountain Lake Airport (PMLA) located in Groveland, California. The PLMA has a residential 
airpark adjacent to it and is considered by the FAA to have RTTF operations. Tuolumne County 
is interested in obtaining FAA grant funding for improving the PLMA under the new RTTF 
policy. You have been asked to gather information about the recent policy change and develop 
an RTTF agreement. The RTTF agreement will be submitted to the Airport Advisory 
Committee, and if approved, will be considered by the Tuolumne County Board of Supervisors 
for adoption. 
You are also a property owner and resident of the airpark directly adjoining the PMLA. 
You are concerned that you may have a conflict of interest in working on behalf of Tuolumne 
County to develop the RTTF agreement that must be executed by residents in order for the 
PMLA to become eligible for FAA grant funding. In essence, you will develop the required 
RTTF access agreement for PMLA and also will be a resident who will agree to the terms of the 
agreement. You note that one of the FAA's requirements is that each resident pay an access fee 
for the privilege of accessing the airport and your responsibilities may require your input on fee 
amount and type. You ask specifically about conflicts of interest at the following stages: 
(1) Gathering publically accessible information pertinent to the project, 
(2) Meeting with agency staff to develop policy options for a potential agreement, 
(3) Drafting proposed agreements, 
(4) Presenting the proposed agreements and policy to your Board of Supervisors as the 
decision maker, 
(5) Executing an agreement, as a resident, to maintain your private access to the airport. 
2 According to the FAA website, "Residential through-the-fence (RTTF) agreements allow people who 
own residential property with aircraft storage facilities near an airport to access the airport from off-airport property. 
These properties are sometimes known as 'hangar homes.' On February 14, 2012, the FAA Modernization and 
Reform Act of 2012 was signed into law (P.L. 112-95). Section 136 of this law permits general aviation airports, as 
defined by the statute, to enter into residential through-the-fence agreements with property owners or association 
representing property owners."

You stated that you would be: (1) gathering publically accessible information pertinent

3 Although you describe two layers of approval of the agreement (the advisory committee and board of

interpretation that [ an official] participates in a decision, even if it is 'reviewed• by several of his superiors, if those 
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the [official] without checking it independently. if they rely on the 
professional judgment of the (official], or if the [official] in some other way actually may intluence the final 
decision. Making recommendations to the final decision-makers, either directly or indirectly, is exactly what

conflict of interest under the "legally required participation .. exception. This

QUESTION: Are you prohibited from working on the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) 
residential through-the-fence-required-access agreement (RTTF agreement) between Tuolumne 
County and residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport, when you are one of the 
residents who live near the Pine Mountain Lake Airport who will enter into the RTTF agreement.

CONCLUSION: Because you will be subject to the agreement in your private capacity you may not 
participate in your governmental capacity on the development of an RTTF agreement on behalf  
of Tuolumne County if the financial effect of the decision on your personal interests will be at 
least $250.
